title: Part I: The Brothers
sourceName: Robert Louis Stevenson — The Master of Ballantrae (1889)
paragraphs: The full truth of this extraordinary affair I have never had cause to keep secret, and many details might surprise those who have only heard the legend. I was present at its beginning; I was called back from the dead, as it were, to be present at its end; and I have set down here what I know, in the order that I know it.

The family of Durrisdeer was an old one in Scotland, and in the year 1745, when the Jacobite cause came to its last crisis, the Master of Ballantrae was the elder son and heir apparent. He was a man of thirty—handsome, bold, intelligent, charming, and utterly without scruple. He had all the graces and none of the virtues. He could captivate anyone he chose and had chosen to captivate most people he had ever met. His brother Henry was the opposite: plain, honest, slow, devoted—not without ability, but without any of the fire that made the Master remarkable.

The father—old Lord Durrisdeer—had decided on a course that was prudent if heartless. When the rebellion began, one son would support the Prince and one would support King George, so that whichever side won, the family would be safe. The question was which son would go which way.
They tossed a coin.

The Master—James Durie—won the toss, or rather, won the right to choose. He chose to go with the Prince, because it was the romantic side, because it offered adventure and glory, because Henry's steady reliability meant Henry could hold the estate. He kissed his father's hand and rode off with the Jacobites, and that was the last time the family was ever truly at peace.

James Durie fought at Culloden. He survived—survived everything, survived as he always survived, by a combination of wit and luck and ruthlessness. He escaped to France and was reported dead. And Henry, believing his brother dead, reluctantly married the woman James had been courting—Alison Graeme—because someone had to, and the family needed an heir.
Then James came back.

title: Part II: The Return
sourceName: Robert Louis Stevenson — The Master of Ballantrae (1889)
paragraphs: He arrived at Durrisdeer on a winter evening, as dramatic as a theatrical entrance—which was, of course, entirely deliberate. James Durie understood the value of an entrance.

He was older, thinner, with lines around his eyes that had not been there before. But he was still the Master—still the same careless, brilliant, malevolent creature, only now with something additional: a settled purpose. He had come back to reclaim what he considered his.
Henry met him at the door. Henry, who had managed the estate through the lean years, who had cared for the old father, who had married Alison and had a son. Henry, who was blameless in everything.
'Brother,' said James, with his most charming smile.
'Brother,' said Henry.

After that, for years, they lived under the same roof. It was a sustained torment. James made no direct claim—he could not, legally, since he had been attainted for treason and Henry now held the title. But he made his presence felt in every possible way. He charmed Alison. He played the devoted son to old Lord Durrisdeer. He tormented Henry with small cruelties and public humiliations, all delivered with that brilliant, poisonous smile.
Henry endured. Henry always endured. It was his greatness and his weakness—he endured and endured and endured, and the endurance ate at him, slowly, like acid.

Their enmity came to open violence at last—a duel, with swords, at night in the garden. It was I who witnessed it. Henry ran James through; James fell. We dragged the body away and buried it—or thought we did—and Henry told the world his brother had gone abroad.
But James was not dead.
He had survived, as he always survived. He was found by smugglers, revived, and vanished into the wider world—to India, I believe, and then to America, always working toward his return.

title: Part III: The End
sourceName: Robert Louis Stevenson — The Master of Ballantrae (1889)
paragraphs: Henry Durie had become a broken man. The years of endurance had left their mark. He had his estate, his title, his son, his wife—and he was a grey, haunted wraith who jumped at shadows and spent his evenings alone, listening for sounds that were not there.

When the news reached him that James was alive—that James was in America and coming—Henry packed his family and came too. He could not face James in Scotland, where James had all the advantages of familiarity and charm and local legend. He would go to New York, and then into the wilderness, and lose himself, perhaps, and perhaps lose James as well.
James followed.
I followed both, because I could not leave Henry. He had been good to me in his broken way, and good service earns loyalty even if gratitude is imperfect.

It came to its end in the American wilderness, in the deep winter, far from any human settlement. James had hired Indians to track Henry into the forest. Henry had made a last desperate plan. Both plans failed and succeeded in their own ways.

James was buried in the American earth—buried alive in a cataleptic trance, by his own men, who feared him in death as they had feared him in life. Henry and I dug up the grave when we found it. We opened the coffin. James opened his eyes.
Henry died at that moment. Literally: his heart stopped, at the sight of those eyes opening, and he fell dead beside the grave that contained his brother.
James survived the exposure long enough to look at his brother's body. Then he too died—peacefully, almost, as if whatever force had sustained him all those years suddenly released him.

They were buried together, in the American wilderness, far from Durrisdeer. The local Indians spoke of the place for years afterward, in their own language, as the place where two brothers died at the same moment.

I have set down the truth as I know it. I was there at the beginning. I was there at the end. I can add nothing to what I have told, except that in all my years of service I never witnessed a hatred so sustained, so creative, so inventive in its cruelties, as the hatred that James Durie bore toward his brother.
Henry was a good man. James was the most brilliant man I ever knew. Between them they destroyed each other, and me, and all of us who were caught in their orbit.
God rest them both.
